exits.label: ◂ back out into the Lamplit Quarter
▸ down the lane to the old house being renovated — The House That Remembers

npcs.name: the Lady of the House
npcs.title: waiting, and will not stop
npcs.hail: *(a grey lady sits at the head of a dust-furred table laid for a dinner that will never come, her hands folded, her chin high, keeping a household together by sheer will long after there is any household left to keep)*

npcs.name: Tam
npcs.title: a small ghost, bored of waiting
npcs.hail: *(a little boy drifts a slow circle around a chair he cannot sit in, trailing one translucent hand along its back, sighing the enormous theatrical sigh of a child who has been told to wait quietly for a very, very long time)*

npcs.name: Goodwife Harl
npcs.title: the housekeeper, who stayed
npcs.hail: *(a stout grey shade stands by the cold hearth with a duster she no longer needs, watching the Lady with forty years of loyalty and one terrible withheld truth, and she startles when your living-cold eyes find her — the first in an age to look her way)*

variants.text: *(The little ghost stops his slow circle and looks up at you, and his whole translucent face brightens with the desperate gladness of a bored child who has finally, finally got a new person to talk to.)* …You can *see* me! *(he drifts close, delighted)* Nobody *new* comes! It's just *Mama*, and *Goodwife Harl*, and the *waiting*, and *more* waiting, and Mama says *don't fidget, Tam, Papa's coming*, but I've been *so good* and *so still* for *so long* and Papa is *not* — *(he catches himself, lowers his voice, conspiratorial)* …can I tell you a *secret?* A *grown-up* one? I've not been able to tell *anybody*, because Mama won't *hear* it and Goodwife Harl goes all *funny* when I try. *(huge, hopeful eyes)* You'll *listen*, won't you? You've got the *listening* sort of face. The *cold* sort. The sort that *believes* a fellow.

nodes.speaker: Tam
nodes.text: *(He bobs in the air at your eye level, thrilled to have an audience, swinging his translucent legs.)* Ask me *anything!* *(brightly)* I know *everything* about this house. I've had *ages* to learn it. There's *nothing* else to *do.*

choices.text: "What's the secret, Tam? The grown-up one you can't tell anybody."
"What are you all waiting for? Your mama keeps saying 'Papa's coming.' Coming from where?"

[INSIGHT] "You're not frightened, and you're not sad — you're *bored*, which means you don't think anything's *wrong*, only *stuck.* A child knows when a house is haunted by horror and when it's just haunted by a grown-up who won't *move.* Which is this, Tam?"

*(gently, one who knows the bright door, to one who's been kept from it)* "Tam — the door you're all waiting by. The one your mama won't let you go up the stairs to. I've been through doors like that. It's not somewhere to be *afraid* of. Tell me what you've seen of it, and I'll tell you true what's on the other side."
"Be good a little longer, Tam. I'm going to sort this out."

nodes.speaker: Tam
nodes.text: *(He drifts very close, and drops his voice to the gravest whisper a small ghost can manage.)* …*Papa already went up.* *(he nods, solemn)* Up the *stairs.* To the *bright door.* I *saw* him. *Ages* ago — right at the *start*, when we all — when it all went *dark* and then there was the *bright door* at the top of the stairs, all *warm.* *(he glances at the grey Lady, lowers his voice further)* Papa *went.* He looked *back*, and he looked *sad*, and he held out his *hand* for Mama — but Mama was *busy.* She was — *(he frowns, trying to remember)* — she was *counting* something. *Candles*, or *coins*, or — she's *always* counting. And by the time she *finished*, Papa had *gone up*, and the door went *quiet*, and Mama said *'he's gone ahead to make ready, we'll follow when it's proper'* — *(his small face crumples)* — and that was *so long ago*, and we *never* follow, because Mama says it's *not proper yet*, and I *told* her Papa *went without us* but she just says *'hush, Tam, don't tell stories.'* *(he looks at you, pleading)* It's *not* a story. I *saw* him *go.* Why won't she *believe* me? We could *follow.* The door's *still there.* I can *feel* it, up the stairs, all *warm.*

nodes.speaker: Tam
nodes.text: *(He sighs, the whole weight of a child's exasperation in it.)* That's the *thing.* *(flatly)* Papa's *not* coming, because Papa's already *gone* — *up*, ahead, through the *bright door*, I *keep saying* — but Mama's got it *backwards.* She thinks we're waiting for him to *arrive.* *(he throws up translucent hands)* We're not waiting for him to *come.* We're waiting because she won't *follow* where he *went.* *(quietly)* It's like — you know when a grown-up's *sure* about a thing, *so* sure, and they're *wrong*, and you *can't* tell them because being a *child* means your *true* things don't *count* as much as their *wrong* things? *(he looks at you)* It's *that.* But *forever.* Mama is *waiting in the wrong direction*, and I'm *stuck* waiting *with* her, and Goodwife Harl *won't leave Mama*, so we're *all* stuck, because *one* grown-up is *certain* and *wrong* and *nobody can move the house without moving her.*

nodes.speaker: Tam
nodes.text: *(He brightens — *thrilled* to be understood — and nods so hard he bobs.)* *Stuck!* *(delighted)* *Yes!* That's the *word*, that's the *exact* word, you *clever* cold thing! *Not* wrong — *STUCK!* *(he does a slow translucent loop of joy)* It's not a *scary* house. *I'm* not *scared.* Nothing *bad* is *here.* It's just — *Mama got stuck*, like a *cart-wheel* in a *rut*, and the *whole house* is the *cart*, and it *can't* go *anywhere* until somebody *lifts the wheel out.* *(he drifts close, earnest)* And *grown-ups* can't lift it, because the *rut* is a *grown-up* thing — *guilt*, Goodwife Harl says, when she thinks I'm not *listening*, *'the poor Lady's guilt'* — and you can't *argue* a grown-up out of *guilt*, I've *tried.* *(he looks at you, hopeful)* But *you're* not *really* a grown-up, are you. You're a *cold* thing that walks through *doors.* Maybe *you* can lift the *wheel.* Maybe you can *un-stick* my *Mama.* *(quietly)* I'd *like* to go up the stairs. I've been *so* good. *So* still. I'd like to *stop* being good now, and just — *go.* With *everybody.* All *together.* That's *allowed*, isn't it? To go *together?*

nodes.speaker: Tam
nodes.text: *(His eyes go round, and he forgets to bob, hanging very still in the air the way children do when a grown-up finally takes their most important thing *seriously.*)* …You've *been* through one. *(hushed)* A *bright door.* *(he drifts close)* Then you *know.* It's *warm*, isn't it. Not *scary.* *Warm*, like — like the *kitchen* used to be, when Goodwife Harl baked, and the whole house smelled of it. *(wistful)* That's what the door at the top of the stairs *feels* like. *Warm*, and *waiting*, and a *little* bit like Papa's *hand.* *(he looks at you, and there's no fear in it at all, only a child's clean longing)* I'm *not* afraid to go up. I was *never* afraid. *(quietly)* I just don't want to go *without Mama.* That's the *only* thing. If I go up the *warm* stairs and *Mama's* still down here in the *cold* counting *candles* — *(his small face works)* — then it's *not* warm, is it. Not *really.* It's just *me*, being *warm*, and *missing* her. *(fiercely, for a small ghost)* So *fix* Mama *first*, cold one. *Then* we'll *all* go up, *together*, and it'll be *properly* warm, the way warm's *supposed* to be. *Promise* you'll do *Mama* first.

nodes.speaker: Tam
nodes.text: *(He shrugs, a big theatrical heave of translucent shoulders, already half-distracted.)* I *dunno.* *(he resumes his slow circle of the chair)* It's not *scary*, if that's what you mean. Nothing *jumps* out. *(a sigh)* It's just *boring.* *So* boring. *Forever* boring. *(he peers at you)* Are *you* boring? You don't *look* boring. You look like *something happens* near you. *(hopeful)* Does something *happen* near you? Because I would *like* something to *happen.* I've been *waiting* for something to *happen* for — *(he loses the thread)* — for *ages.* *Ask* Goodwife Harl the *grown-up* questions. She *knows* the *boring* answers. I just know it's *boring*, and that I *miss* Papa, and that Mama *won't listen.* That's *my* whole list.

nodes.speaker: Tam
nodes.text: *(He droops, the brief brightness of having someone to talk to fading back toward the long grey boredom.)* …Aw. *(he resumes his slow circle of the chair he cannot sit in)* You're *going.* *(a sigh that could move curtains, if curtains moved for ghosts)* Everybody goes. Down the *stairs* and *out*, never *up* the stairs where the *warm* is. *(quietly, hopeful in spite of himself)* …You'll come *back*, though? You said you'd *sort it out.* The *Mama* thing. *(he brightens a fraction)* I'll be *here.* I'm *always* here. *Being good.* *(barely)* …Hurry, though. I've been good for *such* a long time. I should *very* much like to stop, and go *up*, all *together.* Sort out *Mama.* Then come *get* me. *Promise.*

variants.text: *(The stout grey shade clutches her useless duster and looks at you with the wary, hungry expression of a soul who has had no one new to speak to in a very long time and has learned not to trust the wanting of it.)* …You'll be *real*, then. *(quietly)* Or *real* enough to *see* us, which in this house counts for the same. *(she glances at the grey Lady at the head of the table, then back at you, and drops her voice)* I'll tell you *quick*, before my Lady *notices* I'm *talking* — she doesn't *like* it, talk of — of *leaving.* *(she grips the duster)* I'm Harl. Housekeeper. Forty years in this family's service, living *and* — *(a grim almost-laugh)* — *otherwise.* And I *stayed*, when it all went *dark*, because a good housekeeper does *not* leave her Lady, not *living*, not *dead*, not *ever.* *(her grey face creases)* But oh, traveler — *(barely)* — I have *stayed* so *long*, and I am so *tired*, and there is a thing I *know* that would *free* us *all*, and I *cannot say it*, because *saying* it would *break* my Lady's *heart*, and a housekeeper does not *break* her Lady's *heart.* *(she looks at you)* …Unless. Unless *someone else* said it. Someone *not* bound by *forty years* of *service* to keep a *kind* silence. *(her eyes are desperate)* Are you — might you be — *that* someone?

nodes.speaker: Goodwife Harl
nodes.text: *(She keeps her voice low, one eye always on the Lady, the discretion of a lifetime's service outlasting the life itself.)* Ask, then. *Quiet.* And mind — what I tell you, I tell you because I *can't* tell *her*, and *somebody* must *know* it before this house *crumbles* to dust with us *still* in it, *waiting.* *(grimly)* Forty years I kept her *secrets.* It is a *strange* relief to *spend* one at last.

choices.text: "How did it happen, Harl? How did the family die?"

*(if the child told you the husband already crossed)* "Tam says the master already went up — through the bright door, at the very start, and the Lady was too busy counting to follow. Is the boy right? Is she waiting for a man who's already gone?"

[INVESTIGATION] *(study the cold hearth, the dust, the table laid for a dinner that never came)* "This house tells its own story if you read it. The hearth's been cold longer than the rest of the room is dusty. The table's set for a meal nobody started. Something happened *fast*, at dinner, by the fire. Show me where, Harl — the *truth* of the night, not the Lady's version."

[INSIGHT] "The thing you can't say — it's not just *how* they died. It's that the Lady blames *herself*, and you know she's *wrong* to. You've watched her serve a sentence for a crime she didn't commit, for who knows how long, and your *loyalty* won't let you correct your mistress. That silence is killing all of you. Say it to *me.* Let me carry it."

*(one keeper of the unfinished to another)* "You stayed out of loyalty, Harl — you wouldn't leave your Lady. I understand that better than you'd think; I'm a thing that *can't* quite leave either, that walks both sides of the door. So let me make you an offer no living caller could: tell me the truth you've guarded, and I'll carry it across the one gap your loyalty won't let *you* cross — and when this house finally goes up the warm stairs, *you* go with it. Released. At last."
"Keep the watch a little longer, Harl. I'll be back."

nodes.speaker: Goodwife Harl
nodes.text: *(She closes her grey eyes, and the duster goes still in her hands.)* …*Fire.* *(quietly)* A winter's night, the family at dinner — my Lady, the master, little Tam, and the babe in the nursery above. *(her voice roughens)* It started in the *master's study*, next the dining room. *Fast.* The old house went up like *kindling*, and the smoke came *under the door* while they *ate*, and by the time they *knew* — *(she stops, swallows)* — it was the *stairs* or the *windows*, and the *stairs* were *flame*, and — *(barely)* — none of us *made* it out. Not the master, not my Lady, not the *boy*, not the *babe.* Not *me*, who'd gone *up* to fetch the little one and *never* came *down.* *(she opens her eyes, wet)* And my Lady has spent every *moment* since — *living*'s memory and *death*'s long *after* — *certain* it was *her* doing. *Certain.* She'll tell you, if you ask her, in that *high* steady voice: *'I snuffed the hearth-guard. To save the cost of the iron's wear. I was economizing. I killed my family for a* ha'penny *of* iron.'* *(Harl grips the duster)* …And that, traveler, is the *secret* I cannot *say.* Because she is *wrong.* It was *not* the hearth.

nodes.speaker: Goodwife Harl
nodes.text: *(Her grey face crumples, and she presses the duster to her mouth.)* …The boy *saw* that? *(barely)* Oh, the *poor* mite. *(she nods, slow and grieved)* He's *right.* Of course he's *right* — children *see* the doors *clearest*, they've less *guilt* to *cloud* them. *(she glances at the Lady)* The master *went up.* At the *very* first — I *saw* it too, from the *landing*, before the smoke took *me.* The *bright* door opened at the top of the *burning* stairs, and the master stepped *through*, and he *reached back* for my Lady — *(her voice breaks)* — and she would *not* take his hand, because she was *down* on the dining-room floor, in the *smoke*, *counting* the *silver*, trying to *save* it, *certain* she could still set the *household* to rights if she just — *(Harl shakes her head)* — and the door *waited*, and *waited*, and *she would not stop counting*, and at last it went *quiet*, with the master *through* and the *rest* of us *still* in the *dark.* *(she meets your eyes)* He's *gone ahead*, traveler. *Years* ago. To the *warm.* And my Lady sits at that table *certain* he is *coming back*, because she *cannot* bear that he went *without* her — *cannot* bear that her *last* act was to choose the *silver* over his *hand.* *(barely)* The boy has it *exact.* We are waiting in the *wrong direction.* And I *cannot* be the one to *say* so.

nodes.speaker: Goodwife Harl
nodes.text: *(She stares at you as you read the cold hearth, the untouched iron guard still latched across it, and something between terror and an enormous, decades-deferred relief breaks across her grey face.)* …You *see* it. *(a whisper)* The *hearth-guard.* It's *latched.* It is *still latched*, after all this time — because my Lady *never did* snuff it. *(she presses both hands to her mouth)* She *remembers* snuffing it, because she *meant* to, because she'd *fretted* over the *iron's wear* at dinner, *scolded* the master about the *cost* — but the *babe* cried, and she went to the *nursery* stairs, and she *never* came *back* to the *hearth*, and the *guard* stayed *latched*, *safe*, *cold.* *(her voice shakes)* The fire was *never* hers. It was the *master's* study — his *reading-lamp*, knocked in the *night*, oil across the *papers* — a thing *no* soul could have *stopped*, least of all a Lady *fussing over a ha'penny two rooms away.* *(she looks at the latched guard, then at you, weeping)* I have *known* this for the *whole* of our long *after.* I have *dusted* that *latched* guard a *thousand* times, *screaming* it in my *head* — *'it wasn't you, my Lady, it was never you, the guard is LATCHED'* — and *never* once *said* it *aloud*, because she did not *ask*, and a housekeeper does not *correct* her Lady *un-asked.* *(barely)* You *asked.* You *read* it. Now *somebody else* knows the *guard is latched.* Oh, *thank* the gods. *Thank* the gods, it is *out* of me at *last.*

nodes.speaker: Goodwife Harl
nodes.text: *(She sags against the cold hearth, and the duster slips from her grey fingers, and forty years of kept silence finally cracks straight down the middle.)* …*Yes.* *(barely)* That's it. That's the *whole* of it. *(she presses a hand to her chest)* She blames *herself*, and she is *wrong*, and I have *known* she is wrong since the *night it happened*, and my *loyalty* — my *good*, *faithful*, *forty-year* loyalty — has kept me *silent* as a *grave*, and the *silence* has *walled* us *all* in this *weeping* house, *waiting* on a *guilt* that was *never* hers to *carry.* *(she looks at you, wretched)* Do you *see* the *cruelty* of it? I stayed out of *love.* I kept my *peace* out of *respect.* And my *love* and my *respect* have been the *very bars* of the *cage*, because the *one* thing that would *free* her is the *one* thing a *good* servant *cannot* say: *'My Lady, you are* wrong*, and you have grieved a* lie *for a* lifetime.'* *(she grips your cold sleeve)* But *you* are *not* her servant. You owe her *no* silence. *You* can *say* it. *(desperate, hopeful)* Find the *proof* — the *hearth*, the *latched* guard, the *master's* study — and *say* it to her *plain*, the way *I* never *could*, and *maybe* — *maybe* — the *house* can *stop weeping*, and we can *all* go up the *warm* stairs *together*, the way we *should* have *years* ago. *Please.* Be the *unkind* truth a *kind* silence *couldn't.*

nodes.speaker: Goodwife Harl
nodes.text: *(She draws herself up, and the forty years of discretion close over her like a lid.)* …I'll not *gossip* about my Lady's *griefs* to a *stranger*, however *cold.* *(stiffly)* It is not a *housekeeper's place.* *(but her grey eyes flick, desperate, to the latched hearth-guard, and away)* If you've *questions* about this house, you may *put* them *plainly*, and I'll answer what's *proper.* But I'll not have you *prying* at my Lady's *heart* on a *guess.* *(quietly, and the loyalty wars with the longing)* …She has *suffered* enough *prying.* Mostly her *own.* Come back when you've *looked* with more than a *passing* eye. The *house* will *tell* you, if you *read* it *honest.* I'll not do the *reading* *for* you. That much *loyalty* I've *left.*

nodes.speaker: Goodwife Harl
nodes.text: *(She looks at you a long moment — at the cold on you, the both-sides-of-the-door of you — and something in her vast, dutiful exhaustion lets go a notch it has held for forty years.)* …A thing that *can't* quite *leave* either. *(quietly)* Aye. Then you *do* understand. *(she grips the duster, and then, deliberately, sets it down on the cold mantel — a small thing, an enormous thing)* …I'll *tell* you. *(barely)* The whole of it — the *latched* guard, the *master's* lamp, the Lady *blameless* — all the truth my *loyalty* has *throttled* for an *age.* *(her grey eyes fill)* Because you're *right*, traveler: my *staying* was never the *kindness* I told myself. It was a *cage* I built and then *locked us both inside*, out of *love*, and called it *service.* *(she meets your gaze, and there is the beginning of *rest* in it)* Carry it across the gap I *couldn't.* Say it to her *plain.* And *yes* — *(her voice cracks)* — when this house goes *up* at last, I will go *with* it, *released*, the *watch* finally *handed on* to a soul that *can* finish what *loyalty* left *undone.* *(softly)* Forty years, traveler. I have *dusted* this *grief* for *forty years.* …Let me *put the duster down.* You've just told me I'm *allowed.*

nodes.speaker: Goodwife Harl
nodes.text: *(She nods, and stoops, with the ache of forty dutiful years, to pick the duster back up.)* Aye. Go, then. *(quietly)* I'll keep the watch. I always *keep* the watch — it's the *one* thing I'm *for.* *(she runs the useless duster along the cold mantel)* …But *come* back, traveler. *(barely)* You've the *look* of a soul that *finishes* things, and this house has been *un-finished* for so *very* long. *(she meets your eyes, the longing breaking through the loyalty for just a moment)* I am *so* tired of *dusting* a *latched guard.* *(softly)* …Find the *truth* of it. And come *say* it. I'll have the *door* — *(a grim almost-smile)* — well. I'll have *left* the door for *you.* It's the *least* a *housekeeper* can do for the soul who might *finally* let her *rest.*

variants.text: *(The grey Lady inclines her head to you with the flawless, frozen courtesy of a hostess receiving a caller, and does not for one instant let the courtesy slip, because the courtesy is the last thing holding her together.)* …You'll forgive the *state* of the house. *(her voice is high and steady and terrible)* We are *between* staff, and my husband is — *(the smallest catch)* — *delayed.* He'll be *down* directly. He has gone *ahead* to *make ready*, you understand, and when all is *proper* we shall *follow*, the *whole* household, *together*, as is *fitting.* *(she gestures at the dust-furred table, laid for a dinner decades cold)* Do sit. We *keep* a *good* table here. We have *always* kept a *good* table. *(her folded hands tighten, just slightly)* I am *most* particular about the *household.* One *must* be. A house runs on *care*, and *economy*, and a *firm* hand — *(her voice wavers, and steadies by force)* — and I have *kept* this one *running*, through *every* difficulty, and I shall keep it running *until my husband comes down*, however *long* that — however long it *takes.* *(she meets your eyes, and behind the courtesy is a drowning thing)* …Have you, perhaps, *seen* him? On the *road? A tall man, kind eyes — he would have gone* up*, you see. Ahead. To make ready. He'll be down* directly*.*

nodes.speaker: the Lady of the House
nodes.text: *(She sits with her chin high and her hands folded, presiding over a household of dust and ghosts with a composure that has not cracked in longer than you can guess, because she has decided it never will.)* Was there something you *wanted*, caller? *(coolly courteous)* I have a *household* to *keep*, and a *husband* to *await*, and I do *not*, I confess, have a *great* deal of patience left for — *(a flicker)* — for those who come to tell me how *long* I have *waited.* I am *aware* how long I have waited. I *keep* the *count.* *(her hands tighten)* I keep *every* count. Ask your question, and let me *return* to my *waiting.*

choices.text: [PERSUASION] *(if you've found the truth of the fire)* "Lady — your husband isn't coming down, because he didn't go down. He went *up.* Through the bright door, the night of the fire, and he reached back for your hand. You're not waiting for him to *arrive.* You're refusing to *follow* where he *went* — because you can't forgive yourself for the hand you didn't take."

*(if you found the latched guard)* "The hearth-guard is latched, Lady. It's *still* latched. You never snuffed it — you *meant* to, you *fretted* about the iron, but the babe cried and you went to the stairs and you *never came back to the hearth.* The fire was the master's study. His reading-lamp. It was *never* yours. You've grieved a crime you didn't *commit.*"

[INSIGHT] "You keep talking about the household — the economy, the firm hand, the good table. You're still *running* it, Lady, decades after there's a thing left to run, because as long as you're *keeping* the count you don't have to *feel* the thing under it. What are you counting *away?* What happens to you if you *stop?*"

*(gently, one who knows the warm door)* "Lady — I've been up the kind of stairs you're avoiding. The bright door isn't a judgement. It's warm. Your husband's through it, and your son wants to go, and your housekeeper's so tired. You've kept the household beautifully. But a household isn't a building. It's the *people* — and yours are all waiting on *you* to stop counting and take the hand he's still holding out."
"Forgive the intrusion, Lady. I'll see myself out."

nodes.speaker: the Lady of the House
nodes.text: *(The flawless courtesy holds for one heartbeat — two — and then it does not *break* so much as *dissolve*, the way frost dissolves off a windowpane when the sun finally reaches it, and what is underneath is a woman who has been screaming politely for a very long time.)* …He *reached back.* *(barely)* You — how do you — *(her folded hands come apart, and tremble)* …He *reached back*, and I was *counting* the *silver.* *(the high voice cracks all the way through)* The *house* was *burning*, and my *husband* was *holding out his hand* from the *one* door that *mattered*, and I — I thought — I thought *if I could just save the* good *service, the things my mother left me, then the household wouldn't be* ruined*, we could* begin again*, I could set it all to* rights *—* (she presses grey hands to her face) *— and I looked* up*, and the door was* closing*, and his* hand *— his* kind *—* (she cannot finish) *(a long, terrible silence, and when she speaks again it is the smallest voice in the world.)* …I have told myself, all this while, that he went *ahead* to *make ready.* That he is *coming* *down.* Because the *truth* — that he went *without* me, that my *last* act was to choose *forks* over his *hand* — the truth is a thing I *cannot keep house* against. So I have *waited in the wrong direction*, on *purpose*, for *years*, because *waiting* for him to *come* is *bearable*, and *knowing* he *went* is *not.* *(she looks at you, drowning)* …How do I *follow* a man I *let* go *up* *alone? How do I walk through that door and* face *him, when the last thing he saw of me was the* top *of my* head*, bent over the* silver*?*

nodes.speaker: the Lady of the House
nodes.text: *(She goes very still. Her eyes move — for the first time in the conversation, perhaps the first time in the whole long after — to the cold hearth, and the iron guard latched across it, and you watch a certainty she has built her entire death upon meet a fact that will not yield to it.)* …*Latched.* *(a whisper)* It's — *(she rises, drifts to the hearth, and her grey hand passes through the latched guard, again and again, as if she could un-feel it)* …I *snuffed* it. I *know* I snuffed it. I have *known*, every *moment* since, that I *killed* them for the *wear* on a *ha'penny* of *iron* — it is the *bedrock* of me, it is the *thing* I have *built* my *waiting* on — *(her voice shakes)* — but the guard is *latched.* It is *latched.* I *meant* to snuff it, I *scolded* about it, and then *Tam's* sister *cried* in the *nursery* and I — *(she stops, and a memory she has spent a lifetime *refusing* surfaces whole)* — I *went to the babe.* I *never came back to the hearth.* *(she turns to you, and her grey face is naked with a horror worse than guilt: the loss of it)* …Then it was *never* — *(barely)* — it was the *study.* His *lamp.* I was *two rooms away*, fussing over *forks*, and it was *never* — *(she presses both hands to her mouth)* …I have grieved a *murder* I did not *do.* For a *lifetime.* For an *after.* I am *not* the *villain* of this house. I am only — *(the word costs everything)* — I am only its *most* *thorough* *mourner.* *(she looks at you, undone)* …What do I *do*, with a *guilt* I've just *set down?* I do not — I have *carried* it so *long* I do not *know* how to *stand* *without* it.

nodes.speaker: the Lady of the House
nodes.text: *(Her folded hands go white.)* …What do I *count away.* *(the high voice thins)* You think yourself *very* perceptive, caller. *(but she does not deny it; she cannot, in her own house, before her own dead)* …I count *because* if I *stop* — *(a long breath)* — if I stop *keeping* the household, then I am only a *woman* sitting in a *burned* house *full* of the *people I failed*, with *nothing* to *do* but *feel* it. *(she meets your eyes, fierce and frightened)* The *count* is the *one* thing I have *left* that I am *good* at. I was *always* good at it — the *economy*, the *firm* hand, the *good* table — it was my *worth*, my *whole* worth, and it is the *only* worth that *survived* the *fire.* *(barely)* So I *count.* The *silver*, the *candles*, the *days.* Because a woman who is *counting* is a woman who is *useful*, and a *useful* woman is not a woman who *chose* the *forks* over her *husband's* *hand.* *(her composure is in ruins now, held up by nothing)* …Do not ask me to *stop* counting, cold one. *Counting* is the *wall* I have *built* against the *one* night I *cannot* survive *remembering.* If you *take* the *count* — *(she looks at you, drowning)* — you had *better* have something to put in its *place.* Or you will *un-house* me *entirely*, and I do not know *what* is *left* of a soul, when you take away the *last* thing it does *well.*

nodes.speaker: the Lady of the House
nodes.text: *(She turns to you slowly, and the frozen hostess looks at the cold thing that has been to the place she is so afraid of, and something in her reaches for it despite herself.)* …You've *been* up. *(quietly)* The — the *bright* door. *(her folded hands loosen, just slightly)* And it is — you say it is *warm.* Not a *reckoning.* Not a *ledger* opened on a *woman who chose the silver.* Just — *warm.* *(her chin, which has been high for the whole of her long after, dips, the smallest fraction)* …A household is the *people.* *(she repeats it, as though testing a sum she has never run)* Not the *building.* Not the *silver.* The *people.* *(she looks, for the first time, at little Tam drifting his bored circle, and at tired Harl by the cold hearth, and her grey face crumples)* …I have been keeping the *building*, haven't I. All this while. *Polishing* the *forks* of a *house that burned*, while my *son* waited *bored* and my *good* Harl waited *exhausted* and my *husband* waited *through a door I would not walk.* *(barely)* I kept the *wrong* household. I kept the *one* that *died*, instead of the *one* that's *still here, still waiting on me.* *(she looks at you, and there is the beginning of something that is not quite hope but is the *room* where hope goes)* …He's *still* holding out his hand? *Truly?* After I — after the *forks?* *(a whisper)* …Then tell me how, cold one. How a *woman* who chose *wrong* at the *one* door that *mattered* walks *up* to it *now*, and takes the hand she *let go*, and is — *forgiven.* I do not know the *sum* of that. It is the *one* economy I never *learned.*

nodes.speaker: the Lady of the House
nodes.text: *(She stands amid the dust and the ghosts of everyone she loves, the courtesy gone, the count faltering, a woman at the foot of a stair she has refused for a lifetime — and she looks to you, the cold caller who saw the truth, for the one thing she cannot give herself.)* …*Tell* me what to *do.* *(barely)* I have *kept* this house so *long.* I do not know how to *leave* it. Say the thing that lets me *go.*

choices.text: "He didn't reach back to *judge* you, Lady. He reached back because he *wanted you to come.* That hand was never a verdict — it was an *invitation*, and it's *still open.* You don't walk up to be *forgiven.* You walk up because someone who loves you is *holding the door.* The forks don't matter. They never did. Only the hand. Go take it."

"Stop counting the silver — and start counting *them.* One son, bored of waiting. One housekeeper, forty years faithful. One husband, still holding the door. That's *three*, Lady, and they're the only ledger that was ever worth keeping, and every one of them is *waiting on you to total it and go.* Count *that*, and walk."

*(gently)* "You don't have to know how. You only have to take one step toward the stairs, and let the people who love you do the rest. Tam will hold one hand. Harl will steady you. And your husband is at the top, and he has waited this long, and he is *not* going to let go now. Walk, Lady. You're not leaving the household. You're finally *bringing it with you.*"

nodes.speaker: the Lady of the House
nodes.text: *(For a long moment she does not move. And then — slowly, the way a held breath is finally let go — the grey Lady turns from the dust-furred table, and the cold hearth, and the silver she will never count again, toward the foot of the stairs, where a warmth you cannot see but she suddenly, plainly can spills down the steps like late sun.)* …*Oh.* *(barely, wondering)* There it is. *(her hand lifts — toward the top of the stairs, toward a hand only she can see reaching back, still, after all this time)* …He's *still there.* He *waited.* He *waited* the *whole* time, and I thought — I *told* myself he'd gone *ahead* because I could not *bear* that he'd waited, that he'd *want* me, *still*, after the *forks* — *(she laughs, and it is wet, and it is the first un-careful sound she has made in an age)* — *Tam.* *Tam*, come — take my hand, we're — *(the little ghost is already there, delighted, no longer bored, his small hand in hers)* — Goodwife Harl, *leave* the *duster*, leave it, we are *done* keeping *house*, we are going *up* — *(tired Harl drops the duster at last, weeping, forty years of loyalty finally *allowed* to *rest*)* — *(the Lady turns, at the foot of the stairs, and looks back at you, the cold caller who read the latched guard and the held hand and the wrong direction of all her waiting)* …I do not know your *name*, cold one. But you have done a thing for this house that no *living* soul in *decades* of *callers* ever could: you *looked*, and you *read* it *true*, and you said the *unkind* kind thing that *un-stuck* the *wheel.* *(she inclines her head — not the frozen courtesy now, but a real, deep, grateful bow)* The household *thanks* you. *All* of it. The *one* that *lived*, and the *one* that's *leaving.* *(she takes the first step up, into the warm, her son's hand in hers and her housekeeper at her back and her husband's hand reaching down)* …He's *smiling.* Oh — he's *smiling.* He's not *angry* about the *forks* at *all.* *(and the Weeping House, at last, goes quiet, and warm, and empty, the way a house should when everyone has finally gone home together.)*

nodes.speaker: the Lady of the House
nodes.text: *(The courtesy snaps back into place like a portcullis, and her chin lifts, and her voice goes cold and high and final.)* …I do *not* know what *gossip* you have had from my *staff*, caller, but my husband has gone *ahead*, and he is *coming down*, and I will thank you *not* to bring your — your *road-tales* of *bright doors* and *reached-back hands* into a *respectable* house. *(her folded hands tremble, betraying her)* He is *coming down.* He has *always* been *coming down.* *(quietly, and the certainty is a wall with the mortar visibly cracking)* …Say it *again*, if you *must.* But say it *plainer*, and say it *kinder*, for I find I — *(she falters)* — I find the *first* telling did not quite — *(she steadies, barely)* — *try again*, caller. If you are *certain.* I am *not* a woman who is moved by a *careless* word. But a *certain* one — *(a whisper)* — a *certain* one I might, at last, be *unable* to *out-wait.*

nodes.speaker: the Lady of the House
nodes.text: *(✦ You do not argue. You simply *describe* it — the burning stair, the bright door, the kind eyes, the hand reaching *back*, the top of her own bent head over the silver — with such *exact* and *merciless* tenderness that the Lady's lifelong composure does not crack; it is *lifted clean away*, like a sheet off a mirror, and she sees herself, at last, *whole*, in the one moment she has spent an age refusing to look at.)* *(She makes a sound that has no dignity in it at all, and it is the most alive she has been since the fire.)* —*his HAND* — *(she is on her feet, and the courtesy is *gone*, all of it, forever)* — I *let go his HAND* for a *box of FORKS*, and I have spent my whole *DEATH* pretending he went *AHEAD* because the *alternative* was to *KNOW* I *chose the SILVER* — *(she rounds on the stairs, on the warmth spilling down them, on the hand she can suddenly, unbearably, see)* — *(barely)* — *and he is STILL THERE.* *(a whisper)* He is *still* holding it *out.* After the *forks.* After the *years.* After all my *careful*, *cowardly*, *counting* *waiting.* *(she presses her hands to her face)* …*Oh*, you *terrible*, *merciful* thing. You have *broken* the *one* sum I could not *solve*, and the *answer* was *here* the *whole time*, at the *top* of the *stairs*, with *kind eyes*, *waiting.* *(she is already moving, already calling for Tam, for Harl, already *going*)* …*Thank* you. *Thank* you. I will *take* his *hand* this time. I will take it *and never count anything again.*

nodes.speaker: the Lady of the House
nodes.text: *(💀 You reach for the gentle revelation and, in your earnestness, *over-reach* — you lead with the accusation instead of the love, *'you chose the silver over your husband, Lady, and you know it'* — and the truth, true as it is, lands as a *blow* instead of a *key*, and the Lady flinches back into her composure like a creature into its shell, and the shell, you realise too late, is *thicker* now for having been *struck.*)* *(Her voice is glacial.)* …*Get out.* *(barely controlled)* I do not *know* what *cruelty* sends a soul into a *grieving* house to fling such — such *filth* at a *Lady* in her *own* — *(her folded hands are shaking, but her chin is iron)* …*My husband is coming down.* *(flat, final, the wall rebuilt and reinforced)* He is *coming down*, and when he *does*, I shall tell him a *cold stranger* came and tried to tell me I *killed* him with a *box of forks*, and he shall *laugh*, and we shall *follow* together when it is *proper*, and *not* one *moment* sooner, and *certainly* not on the *say-so* of *you.* *(quietly, and the grief is sealed back under the ice, deeper than before)* …Go. And do not *come back* with your *truths* until you have *learned* that a *truth* flung like a *stone* is just a *stone.* I have been *struck* with *quite* enough of *those.* Mostly by my *own* hand. I did *not* need *yours.*

nodes.speaker: the Lady of the House
nodes.text: *(She receives your insight with a serene, frozen smile, and folds her hands more firmly.)* How *novel*, to be *analysed* in one's *own* drawing-room. *(coolly)* I *count*, caller, because a *household* requires *counting*, and I *keep* a *good* house. That is not a *symptom.* It is a *virtue.* *(but her eyes do not quite meet yours)* You'll forgive me if I do not *unburden* my *soul* to a passing *stranger* on the strength of a *clever* guess. *(quietly)* If you wish to be of *use* in this house, caller, do not *theorise* at the *Lady.* *Look* at the *house.* It *keeps* its *truths* in *plainer* sight than my *heart* does. *Read* it, and come *back* when you've something *firmer* than a *feeling.* I have *out-waited* *feelings* before. I have out-waited *everything.* It is, I am told, my *one* great *talent.*

nodes.speaker: the Lady of the House
nodes.text: *(She inclines her head, the flawless hostess once more, and the courtesy seals shut over the drowning thing beneath it.)* Of course. *(coolly serene)* Do mind the *step* on your way out; the house is not as *kept* as I should *like*, between staff. *(she folds her hands, and returns her gaze to the foot of the stairs she will not climb, and the door she will not name)* …Should you *pass* a tall man on the road — *kind* eyes — *(the smallest catch, instantly mastered)* — do tell him his *wife* is *keeping* his *table*, and *waits* on him, and *will* wait, however *long* it — *(she steadies)* — *thank* you for *calling.* We so *rarely* receive, these days. *(quietly, as you reach the door, almost to herself)* …He's *coming* down. He has *always* been *coming* down. One *must* simply *keep* the house, until he *does.*
